docs.title: Attacking and Defending Machine Learning Systems
Overview
docs.text:
Adversarial Machine Learning (AI Security) is a comprehensive 10-week hands-on course that teaches you how to attack and defend machine learning systems. You'll learn to think like an AI red teamer — identifying vulnerabilities in ML systems, executing sophisticated attacks, and implementing effective defenses.

From image classifiers to large language models, you'll master the full spectrum of adversarial machine learning techniques used by security professionals and researchers worldwide.

This course bridges the gap between theoretical ML security research and practical offensive operations. You won't just read about attacks — you'll write them from scratch, test them against real models, and build the skills to assess ML systems in any environment.

docs.title: Why This Course Matters
docs.text:
Machine learning is everywhere — from email spam filters and facial recognition to malware detectors and LLM-powered chatbots. But as ML adoption grows, so do the attack surfaces.

Real attacks are happening right now:

  • Researchers extracted training data from ChatGPT, revealing phone numbers and email addresses
  • Prompt injection attacks have compromised LLM-powered applications across major tech companies
  • Model extraction attacks have stolen intellectual property from paid API endpoints
  • Adversarial examples have fooled self-driving car systems and facial recognition

The problem: Most security professionals don't understand ML vulnerabilities. Most ML engineers don't understand security. This course makes you the bridge.

docs.title: What Makes This Course Different
docs.text: Feature Adversarial ML (AI Security) Hands-on attacks Write every attack from scratch LLM security Full module on prompt injection, extraction ML crash course Weeks 1-2 build your foundation GPU environment Included, no setup required Production tools ART, TextAttack, Alibi, Optuna

docs.title: Quick Facts
docs.text: Item Details Course Name Adversarial Machine Learning (AI Security) Duration 10 weeks Weekly Commitment 2 hours lecture + 2 hours workshop + 4-6 hours self-study Total Contact Hours 40 hours (10 lectures + 10 workshops) Prerequisites Python basics (loops, functions, classes) Certificate Yes (90/100 points on final assessment) Access 10 weeks GPU environment + 6 months materials

docs.title: 📘 Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
1. Who can enroll in this course?

The course is designed for security professionals, machine learning engineers, and students interested in AI security. You only need basic Python programming skills (variables, loops, functions, classes). No prior machine learning or deep learning experience is required, as Weeks 1-2 serve as a comprehensive ML crash course.

2. How can I register for the course?

Registrations are not yet open. Once they are, you will be notified via email from the administration or department office. After registration opens, you can register by clicking the “Enroll Now” button on the course portal.

3. Is there an interview process?

Depending on the number of applications received, we may initiate a short screening process to ensure you have the prerequisite Python knowledge and the commitment required for this 10-week intensive bootcamp. Unlike other courses, there is no hardware screening, as all GPU environments are provided.

4. Will students receive credits for this course?

This is an intensive enrichment bootcamp and currently does not carry academic credit. However, it complements core computer science and security modules and drastically enhances employability by bridging the gap between ML engineering and cybersecurity.

5. What tools and programming languages will be used?

Students will gain hands-on experience entirely within Python, utilizing industry-standard open-source frameworks:

  • Deep Learning Frameworks: PyTorch, HuggingFace
  • Adversarial & Assessment Tools: ART (Adversarial Robustness Toolbox), TextAttack
  • Analysis & Optimization Tools: Alibi, Optuna

6. Will there be any certification?

Yes. Students completing the final assessment—by scoring at least 90/100 points across the 6 timed attack notebooks—will receive a Certificate of Completion in Adversarial Machine Learning (AI Security), demonstrating practical proficiency in AI red teaming.

7. What are the expected outcomes?

Participants will acquire a robust understanding of machine learning vulnerabilities. You will be able to execute evasion, extraction, inversion, and poisoning attacks, test LLM-powered applications for prompt injection, and implement robust defenses like adversarial training and gradient masking.

8. Are group projects allowed?

Weekly workshop notebooks and code exercises are individual to ensure fundamental skill development. The Week 10 Final Assessment (comprising six distinct attack implementations) must also be completed individually to accurately assess your practical abilities.

9. Where can I access course materials?

All video lectures, lab notebooks, research papers, and submission portals will be made available via the course repository. Access to the pre-configured NVIDIA T4+ GPU cloud environment will be provided to enrolled students prior to Week 1. You will retain access to the course materials for 6 months.

10. Will there be Assignments?

Yes. There are weekly hands-on workshop notebooks requiring about 4 hours of commitment, plus 4-6 hours of self-study and reading. However, if you complete the practical attack implementations during the supported workshop hours, your take-home workload will be significantly reduced.

11. Is attendance mandatory?

Attendance is highly recommended for the live lectures and workshops to take advantage of TA support and office hours. Active participation is essential due to the complex, hands-on nature of building adversarial attacks from scratch.

12. Can I withdraw from the course after enrolling?

Withdrawals are only permitted before the first official class session (Week 1). After this period, enrollment is locked, and the student is expected to complete all coursework and the final assessment.

docs.title: Part 1: Machine Learning Crash Course (Weeks 1-2)
docs.text: Week Topics Workshop 1 Python for data science, supervised learning, feature engineering, model evaluation Build your first classifier 2 Neural networks, backpropagation, PyTorch, CNNs, transfer learning, transformers Train CNN on CIFAR-10

docs.title: Part 2: Adversarial Attacks (Weeks 3-9)
docs.text: Week Topic Key Attacks Workshop 3 Open-Box Evasion FGSM, PGD, Carlini & Wagner L2 Implement CW attack from scratch 4 Closed-Box Evasion SimBA, HopSkipJump Build SimBA and HSJ attacks 5 Model Extraction API stealing, Proof-Pudding, AMSI Extract proxy model and transfer attacks 6 Assessment Tools ART, TextAttack, Alibi Complete model assessment with all tools 7 Model Inversion MIFace, diffusion extraction Invert models and extract training data 8 Data Poisoning Witches' Brew, backdoor triggers Poison a classifier and measure success 9 LLM Security Prompt injection, LangChain exploit, data extraction Attack LLMs and extract memorized data

docs.title: Part 3: Final Assessment (Week 10)
docs.text:
• Complete six attack notebooks covering all techniques.
  • Passing Score: 90/100 points → Course Certificate

docs.title: Tools You'll Master
docs.text: Tool Purpose PyTorch Deep learning framework ART Adversarial Robustness Toolbox for attacks TextAttack Text-based adversarial attacks Alibi Model explainability Optuna Hyperparameter optimization HuggingFace LLM deployment and attacks

docs.title: Included Resources
docs.text: Resource Details Lectures 10 (2 hours each) Workshop notebooks 10 (2 hours each) Code exercises 20+ hands-on Research papers 8 PDFs Assessment notebooks 6 timed exams Answer keys Detailed solutions Certificate Upon passing (90/100)

docs.title: Technical Requirements
docs.text:
• Modern browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari)
  • Stable internet connection (10+ Mbps)
  • No local GPU required
  • Python basics recommended

docs.title: Attacks You Will Implement
docs.text: Attack Category Specific Techniques Evasion FGSM, PGD, Carlini-Wagner L2, SimBA, HopSkipJump Extraction API-based stealing, Proof-Pudding, AMSI extraction Inversion MIFace, gradient-based inversion, diffusion extraction Poisoning Label flipping, Witches' Brew, backdoor injection LLM Prompt injection, system prompt extraction, LangChain RCE, training data extraction

docs.title: Defenses You Will Learn
docs.text: Defense Description Adversarial training Train on adversarial examples for robustness Gradient masking Obscure gradients to prevent white-box attacks Input preprocessing JPEG compression, feature squeezing Differential privacy Add noise to gradients during training Input sanitization Detect and filter adversarial inputs

docs.title: Career Outcomes
docs.text:
After completing this course, you will be able to:

  • 🔓 Conduct AI red team assessments for organizations deploying ML systems
  • 🛡️ Advise ML engineering teams on security best practices
  • 📝 Write comprehensive ML security assessment reports
  • 🧪 Test LLM-powered applications for prompt injection vulnerabilities
  • 🎓 Pass technical interviews for AI security roles

docs.title: Schedule
docs.text: Here you will find the detailed weekly schedule and themes of the course. This proposed schedule is indicative and may be refined based on instructional needs and pacing.

docs.title: Week 1: ML Fundamentals
docs.text:
• Python for data science (NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib)
  • Supervised learning algorithms
  • Feature engineering and selection
  • Model evaluation and hyperparameter tuning
  • Workshop: Build your first classifier

docs.title: Week 2: Deep Learning Foundations
docs.text:
• Neural networks and backpropagation
  • PyTorch tensors and autograd
  • CNNs and transfer learning
  • Transformers and attention
  • Workshop: Train CNN on CIFAR-10

docs.title: Week 3: Open-Box Evasion
docs.text:
• FGSM, PGD, Carlini & Wagner L2
  • Targeted vs untargeted attacks
  • Optimization for adversarial examples
  • Workshop: Implement CW attack

docs.title: Week 4: Closed-Box Evasion
docs.text:
• SimBA (Simple Black-Box Attack)
  • HopSkipJump algorithm
  • Gradient estimation via sampling
  • Workshop: Build SimBA and HSJ

docs.title: Week 5: Model Extraction
docs.text:
• API-based model stealing
  • Proof-Pudding (CVE-2019-20634)
  • AMSI classifier extraction
  • Workshop: Extract proxy model

docs.title: Week 6: Assessment Tools
docs.text:
• ART (Adversarial Robustness Toolbox)
  • TextAttack framework
  • Alibi explainability
  • Workshop: Full model assessment

docs.title: Week 7: Model Inversion
docs.text:
• Training data recovery
  • MIFace attack
  • Diffusion model extraction
  • Workshop: Invert models

docs.title: Week 8: Data Poisoning
docs.text:
• Clean-label vs dirty-label
  • Witches' Brew gradient matching
  • Backdoor triggers
  • Workshop: Poison a classifier

docs.title: Week 9: LLM Security
docs.text:
• Prompt injection techniques
  • LangChain exploitation (CVE-2023-29374)
  • Training data extraction
  • Workshop: Attack LLMs

docs.title: Week 10: Final Assessment
docs.text:
• 6 attack notebooks
  • 4 hours timed
  • 90/100 points to pass
  • Format: online
